Firstly, the names from the original databases were interpreted by regional botanists, considering the taxonomic concepts and nomenclature used in the focal region of each database.

Antwortvariable.evidence_anchor: Von den insgesamt 23 Rote-Liste-Arten fanden sich 2016 allein 17 Arten in der Unterabt. 2d, darunter kamen auch alle vier stark gefährdeten Arten (RL 2) nur hier vor: _A. liliago_ (> 1000 Exemplare), _P. cervaria_ (> 100 Exemplare) und _P. odoratum_ (> 100 Exemplare) konzentrieren sich dabei besonders auf den freigestellten steilen SW-Hang unterhalb des Aussichtspunktes. Alle drei Arten blühen hier im Freistand auch reichlich, die Blütenstände von _A. liliago_ werden allerdings stark vom Rehwild verbissen (Abb. 8b). Die drei Arten finden sich auch im angrenzenden Buchenwald, gedeihen dort jedoch meist nur noch vegetativ.

Lebensraum.evidence_anchor: In the mid-1990s, a Spruce Bark Beetle ( Ips typographus L.) outbreak affected the Norway spruce ( Picea abies (L.) Karst.) forests in the higher elevations of the Bavarian Forest National Park in Germany.

Trend.evidence_anchor: Species composition of the predisturbance samples is a significantly nested subset of species composition of postdisturbance samples (SES = 3.31, p = 0.002). Therefore, canopy dieback caused a certain increase in species richness ( V = 8, p < 0.001) due to local establishment of a few light-demanding species already present in the area but was not accompanied by extinction of previously present species.

Antwortvariable.evidence_anchor: However, the impacts of structure and hence stand management often overrule the effects of mixed-species stands on biodiversity. The fact that the effect of mixtures vary with the species or group of species being considered further limits generalizations. For example the diversity of herbs and grasses declines with the introduction of European beech (Fagus sylvatica L.) into Scots pine (Pinus sylvestris L.) forests, while the abundance and number of species of Lumbricids may increase (Ammer et al. 2006).

Antwortvariable.evidence_anchor: Population densities in the native range are usually around 10 – 12 raccoons per 100 ha (Kaufmann 1982) and can reach 333 individuals per 100 ha in urban sites (Riley 1998). Population densities in the non-native range are lower than this, with the highest densities in swamp areas of Northeastern Germany (Müritz National Park) with 6 – 8 individuals per 100 ha (Muschik et al. 2011) and in the urban areas of Bad Karlshafen and Kassel in Central Germany where densities exceed 100 individuals per 100 ha (Hohmann and Bartussek 2011).

Hauptgruppe_RoteListen.evidence_anchor: Raccoons were introduced in different European countries by deliberate or accidental releases occurring since the early twentieth century (Beltrán-Beck et al. 2012). They have become widely established, and are considered a pest in several places due to the economic damage they cause, their threat to public health and negative interactions (competition and predation) with native species (Ikeda et al. 2004; Beltrán-Beck et al. 2012; Vos et al. 2012, 2013). Additionally, they were identified as one of the top ten invasive alien species with the greatest potential to threaten biodiversity in Great Britain (Roy et al. 2014b).

Lebensraum.evidence_anchor: The forested Solling mountains probably provide the most comparable habitat to that typically occupied in the native range, and population densities here are 1 – 4 individuals per 100 ha (Hohmann 1998).

Trend.evidence_anchor: The increase from about 9,000 harvested raccoons in 2000/01 to about 71,000 in 2011/12 reflects the extensive amount of suitable habitat for this omnivorous species in Central Europe.

_2.2. Overview of the forest affinity assessment_


A summary of the forest affinities for all 11883 species represented
in the forest affinity lists (Table 1) reveals that 38.0% of the species
established in Germany (4517 species) are not associated with forest
habitats (category “o”), whereas roughly one third of the fauna (4169
species, 35.1%) occurs in both forest and open habitats (categories “m”,
“mm”, “mo”). This leaves 26.2% (3108 species) of the species which are

Lebensraum.evidence_anchor: Since 2011 a list of the forest affinities of all forest species of vascular plants, bryophytes, and ferns known from Germany, is available. Recently, the first comparable lists of the forest affinities of animal species were published. These lists include a categorization of forest affinities for all species of Crassiclitellata: Lumbricidae (earth worms), Araneae (spiders), Opiliones (harvestmen), Pseudoscorpiones (pseudoscorpions), Hemiptera: Heteroptera (true bugs), Coleoptera (beetles), Hymenoptera: Aculeata (ants, bees, wasps), Lepidoptera: Macrolepidoptera (butterflies, moths partly) and Aves (birds) known from Germany.

Trend.evidence_anchor: The results indicate that open landscape species are particularly threatened. With exception of Lumbricidae (only two threatened species in Germany), species of open landscapes are overrepresented in the group of threatened species in all species groups (Fig. 2). Across all 8 higher taxa, the largest fraction of threatened species is found among species only occurring in open landscapes (O; about 50%). The lowest fraction of threatened species with known forest affinity is found in species predominantly living in forests (F; about 21%). Species occurring in forests and open landscapes but not predominantly in forests (M) show an intermediate percentage of threatened species (about 25%).
